value.Title: Enlight keeps forecast after 50%-plus growth across Q1 fin metrics

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-Y103-S94M-21J3-00000-00Enlight keeps forecast after 50%-plus growth across Q1 fin metrics2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisEnlight keeps forecast after 50%-plus growth across Q1 fin metricsSeeNews

May 7 (Renewables Now) - Israeli renewables developer-operator Enlight Renewable Energy Ltd (TLV:ENLT) on Tuesday reaffirmed its 2026 forecast regardless of a 63% year-on-year drop in first-quarter net profit to USD 38 million (EUR 32.3m).

When excluding a gain from the sale of a 44% stake in the Sunlight cluster of solar and energy storage projects in Israel, the bottom-line result increased by 76% in annual terms. "2026 is off to a strong start, reflected in consistent and impressive over 50% growth across Enlight's financial metrics," CEO Adi Leviatan said.

The Israeli firm closed the first quarter with adjusted EBITDA of USD 154 million, up from USD 132 million a year before. Excluding the Sunlight stake sale and the contribution from an additional deal, adjusted EBITDA rose by 58%.

At USD 200 million, total revenues and income jumped by 54% on the year, fuelled by increased revenues from electricity sales, which improved by 43% to reach USD 156 million. Major contributors to the increased revenues were the Quail Ranch solar-plus-storage park in New Mexico and the Roadrunner complex in Arizona, which Enlight brought online in the last trimester of 2025. The higher output of existing projects and higher power trading activity in Israel also added to the improvement.

The company closed the quarter with a 21.5-GW total generation portfolio and 69 GWh of energy storage assets.

Looking ahead, Enlight reiterated its full-year guidance for revenues of between USD 755 million and USD 785 million, equal to a 32% increase at the midpoint from 2025. Adjusted EBITDA is still seen to be at USD 545 million-565 million, representing a 27% year-on-year increase.

By the end of 2028, the company expects to register a total annual revenues and income run rate of USD 2.1 billion-2.3 billion following the commissioning of projects currently in its mature portfolio.

(USD 1.0 = EUR 0.851)

value.Title: Kit Carson Electric Questa Solar Hydrogen Site Activity May 13, 2026

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-RGF3-S9TS-R3P1-00000-00Kit Carson Electric Questa Solar Hydrogen Site Activity May 13, 20262026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisKit Carson Electric Questa Solar Hydrogen Site Activity May 13, 2026

Key Highlights:

* Early-phase construction and coordination activities underway.

* Project timelines and construction phases to be shared as details confirmed.

* KCEC committed to transparency and community updates.

Original Press Release:

May 13 -- Kit Carson Electric Cooperativeissued the following news release:

Kit Carson Electric Cooperative is aware of public interest regarding activity at the proposed Questa Hydrogen Project site. Preliminary construction and site preparation activities associated with the project's solar infrastructure have begun.

At this stage, work is limited to early-phase construction and coordination activities. Additional information regarding project timelines, construction phases, and community updates will be shared as details are confirmed.

KCEC remains committed to transparency and responsible communication as the project progresses.

About us

Kit Carson Electric Cooperative (KCEC) is a member-owned rural electric utility based in Taos, New Mexico, founded in 1944. It serves roughly 30,000 members across Taos, Colfax, and Rio Arriba counties. KCEC is known for transitioning to renewable energy, focusing on local solar generation, battery storage, and fiber-to-the-home broadband internet.

[Category: Construction and Materials, Renewable Energy, ESG and Sustainability]

Source: Kit Carson Electric Cooperative

value.Title: Community solar is finally shining bright
value.Overview: The Santa Fe New Mexican, A; Pg. 7, 1096words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-7FY3-S2C0-416C-00000-00Community solar is finally shining bright2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisAPg. 7Community solar is finally shining brightNicholas Gilmore ngilmore@sfnewmexican.com

About five years after the New Mexico Legislature passed a law creating the state's community solar program, projects around the state are finally getting off the ground.

Officials, lawmakers and renewable energy advocates have acknowledged the rollout of the state's community solar program has been slower than expected, but in recent months, the program's momentum appears to be increasing as projects materialize.

Community solar proponents believe the last five years have offered lessons for improving the statewide program as it approaches an expansion in capacity that will allow for more projects to be awarded and built.

The state's community solar program allows people, businesses and municipalities to subscribe to solar projects and receive credits toward their utility bills, offering an opportunity for renters and low-income residents to benefit from significant savings and increase New Mexico's solar portfolio.

The most recent community solar project completed is called the Pino Site in Las Vegas, N.M., where several people gathered Wednesday morning to celebrate the project coming online.

The facility, off Seventh Street at the northern end of Las Vegas, is one of eight community solar projects around the state developed by a partnership of Albuquerque-based Pluma Construction, Forefront Power, Standard Solar and Solstice. The projects have developed a total capacity of 48.4 megawatts of solar, including facilities in Las Cruces, Silver City and Clovis, along with others. All of the projects have been completed except one, a facility in Alamogordo, which is expected to be completed by the end of the year.

Chris Pacheco, owner of Pluma Construction, said the community solar program has proved to be a "tremendous growth opportunity" for New Mexico companies like his.

"What this does show is that even if you are a small New Mexico company, if you branch out and partner, you can grow your company," Pacheco said. "I hope this portfolio is remembered not just because of the successful energy projects, but as an example of what can happen when local leadership and strong partnerships come together for the state of New Mexico."

Rep. Tara Lujan, D-Santa Fe, praised the community solar program and the Pino Site at the event.

"One of many reasons why I've become a legislator is to really ensure that our dollars and our economy and our budget are going towards what we see up here today," Lujan said, gesturing at the rows of solar panels behind her.

The projects by the partnership have been among the first to come online from an initial round of community solar awards announced by the Public Regulation Commission in May 2023.

Of the 47 projects - and about 196 megawatts of community solar - chosen by the commission, 13 were online Wednesday, agency spokesperson Patrick Rodriguez said. He noted all of the awarded projects are expected to come online in the next few years, and 39 of them are on track to be complete by the end of 2026.

In recent years, state utility regulators approved an increase to the capacity of the community solar program, meaning more projects will eventually come online.

Cost hurdles

One hurdle for community solar projects getting off the ground has been the cost of interconnection to the electrical grid, which can run into the hundreds of thousands or even into the millions of dollars, said Jim DesJardins, executive director of the Renewable Energy Industry Association ofNew Mexico.

Sometimes those costs are not known by developers upfront, he said, and the interconnection costs can make a project less viable financially.

"If developer 'X' knew that the site that they bid on was going to require a $10 million investment to upgrade a substation, they would have said, 'I think we'll look somewhere else,' but they didn't know that ahead of time," DesJardins said. "So we need to get better at sharing information."

DesJardins' trade group and others have argued for more "visibility" of interconnection costs by utilities in a rulemaking proceeding that is underway at the commission. The process is expected to yield changes to community solar regulations in the coming months as part of an effort improve the program.

Billing rules

Another proposal for the rule change is the concept of consolidated billing, which would eliminate the need for a separate monthly bill for a community solar subscription.

The company Solstice - which manages subscriptions for several community solar projects - is required to send its subscribers in New Mexico a separate invoice from their utility bill, Tyler Yasa, one of the company's executives, said in an interview, adding that under state regulations, customers are kicked off the program after one missed payment.

Yasa described the billing rules as an "existential threat" to New Mexico's community solar program, saying the dual billing system has essentially resulted in his company accepting the default risk from utilities for community solar subscribers, since those subscribers - which are largely low-income - are more likely to relinquish their subscription to community solar than risk losing their power if they are ever unable to pay.

The company was the first to start billing community solar subscribers in New Mexico, he said, and its data shows turnover in subscribers that he attributed to the billing issues.

"Because of how quickly we're churning through customers, we're going to reach a saturation level at some point in the very near future where we can't continue to subscribe these customers, because we've already reached out to the majority of them," Yasa said. "I think in order to make this program as successful as possible and actually have longevity, we have to address the subscriber question - where does that default risk land?"

He pointed out several other states have moved from dual billing to consolidated billing for community solar programs, including Maryland, Illinois and New York.

In the rulemaking proceeding underway at the Public Regulation Commission, commissioners will considering several models for consolidated billing. Solstice and others have pushed for a feature called net crediting, which includes the application of the community solar credit directly on the subscriber's utility bill.

Comments submitted to the commission by a host of advocacy groups - including the Coalition for Community Solar Access and Sierra Club - urged the commission to adopt consolidated billing with net crediting in the coming rule change, calling both features "essential to maintaining the program's low-income participation objectives."

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-9613-S4Y3-M529-00000-00New Mexico deserves land commissioner ready for future2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisAPg. 10New Mexico deserves land commissioner ready for futureMatthew McQueen

As I travel the state campaigning to be the next land commissioner, I spend a lot of time explaining just what the State Land Office is. The State Land Office is the most important agency in New Mexico that most people never think about.

The land office is older than the state ofNew Mexico itself. As a territory and again at statehood, the federal government granted New Mexico lands to be used to fund state institutions. Today those lands, held in trust by the land office for the people of New Mexico, comprise approximately 9 million surface acres and 13 million mineral estate acres.

The land office uses state trust lands to earn money for public institutions, and it does a very good job - earning over $2 billion per year. That money is put into the Land Grant Permanent Fund, which is invested and grows, and is eventually paid out to 21 different beneficiaries, primarily public schools, universities and hospitals. The payments from the Land Grant Permanent Fund save the average taxpayer $3,000 per year.

That's the good news. The bad news is that 97% of that revenue is coming from the oil and gas industry. While we appreciate the contributions of that industry, being so heavily reliant on revenue from a single industry puts the continued growth of the Land Grant Permanent Fund at risk. We also know the oil and gas industry is subject to boom-and-bust cycles, and renewable energy is already out-competing oil and gas on a price-per-kilowatt basis. If we want to continue to grow the Permanent Fund, we must diversify the revenue earned from state trust lands.

The land office has been working hard on other types of revenue-generating projects, and we must continue that effort and build on that success. With proper staffing and in-house expertise, the land office can do more with renewable energy (including wind, solar and geothermal), commercial projects (on appropriate parcels), and affordable and workforce housing (again, on appropriate parcels). I always say "on appropriate parcels" because there are plenty of parcels where the "highest and best use" is in an undeveloped state that benefits outdoor recreation (including hiking, camping, hunting and fishing), wildlife, agriculture and protection of cultural resources. With 9 million acres to work with, we can do it all.

It is also imperative we ensure the oil and gas industry operates responsibly on our state trust lands. The industry must pay a fair price, follow the rules and clean up their own messes. It's that simple.

I am the only candidate in the race with a proven track record of holding the oil and gas industry accountable. I worked to restore the ability of the Oil Conservation Division to levy fines and penalties on bad actors and, with current Land Commissioner Stephanie Garcia Richard and her staff, raised the royalty rate paid by out-of-state oil and gas companies in the Permian Basin.

It is important the next land commissioner understands they are a de facto regulator of the industry. That's why I have not, do not, and will not accept any campaign funds from the oil and gas industry. I am the only candidate to make that commitment.

As a conservation attorney and state legislator - and hopefully your next land commissioner - I will always fight to protect the natural landscapes that we all treasure. You deserve a land commissioner with the proven experience and qualifications to protect our public lands, raise money for our schools and lead the state land office into the future.

Matthew McQueen is a lawyer, legislator and a candidate for state land commissioner.

value.Jurisdiction: New Mexico; Utah
value.Title: Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH1-CFP3-RYYH-G4W7-00000-00Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisTierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fundDylan Ortuno

Tierra Adentro, a New Mexico-based private equity firm, announced May 4 it would be launching and overseeing Tierra Adentro Fund, a $100 million infrastructure fund meant to help technology companies physically build in New Mexico.

"Entrepreneurs thinking about expanding in New Mexico are embracing the model we are building at Tierra Adentro," Managing Partner and Chief Investment Officer Alok Sindher said. "At the end of the day we are helping companies scale in a way traditional equity financing cannot and frankly should not. Founders can instead retain more autonomy and ownership of their company as they grow in, or expand to, New Mexico."

Sindher comes to Tierra Adentro Growth Capital after working for over 20 years in private equity. He was most recently a venture partner for head of infrastructure at Fifth Wall, a San Francisco-based asset manager focused on a mix of real estate and technology, and notably previously served as a Vice President in Investment Banking at J.P. Morgan.

The Tierra Adentro Fund is intended for investments ranging from a few million dollars to $40 million, according to the firm. So far, the company has already invested in geothermal energy developer Zanskar, based out of Wasatch Front in Utah, and expects to close another pair of investments soon.

Tierra Adentro Growth Capital will receive support from Alexander Harstrick and Jonathan Bronson, the founders of Boston-based venture capital firm J2 Ventures. The pair will serve as strategic advisors for the firm.

"The Tierra Adentro fund has more companies seeking investment than it can currently handle - with no marketing to date," Harstrick said. "This fund has the potential to create hundreds of high-wage jobs in the state and will duly ensure the State Investment Council's capital remains squarely focused on developing critical aspects of the state's future."

New Mexico SIC Director for Private Equity and Venture Capital Chris Cassidy said the new fund would benefit the state's technology ecosystem due to the "consolidation of major infrastructure platforms and the significant demand from the next generation of deep tech," the latter term referring to technology focused on global issues or with the ability to drastically change fundamental parts of living.

"The incentives and environment around building in New Mexico are already primed, but this fund's value proposition was missing," Cassidy said. "I am confident it will complement the SIC's broader investment strategy."

The SIC is Tierra Adentro Fund's primary investor. In the last three years, the SIC has committed a total of $1.8 billion to venture funds globally.

value.Title: Long before it was cool, Ted Turner was fighting to save the planet

Document.Content: urn:contentItem:6JHT-6NS3-S27N-Y20X-00000-00<published>2026-05-08T00:00:00Z</published><updated>2026-05-28T16:00:51Z</updated><author><name>LexisNexis</name></author><content type="application/xml"><!--Transformation version 1.25--><articleDoc xmlns="" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="http://www.lexisnexis.com/xmlschemas/content/public/articledoc/1/" schemaVersion="1.8" xml:lang="en"><articleDocHead><itemInfo><sourceSectionInfo><positionSection>MAIN</positionSection><positionSequence>Pg. 1A</positionSequence></sourceSectionInfo></itemInfo></articleDocHead><nitf:body xmlns:nitf="http://iptc.org/std/NITF/2006-10-18/"><nitf:body.head><nitf:hedline><nitf:hl1>Long before it was cool, Ted Turner was fighting to save the planet</nitf:hl1></nitf:hedline><nitf:byline><author><person><nameText>Drew Kann </nameText></person></author><email normalizedValue="mailto:drew.kann@ajc.com">drew.kann@ajc.com</email></nitf:byline><nitf:byline><author><person><nameText>Staff</nameText></person></author></nitf:byline><highlight><bodyText><p>Legacy of conservation, species protection will be felt for generations.</p></bodyText></highlight></nitf:body.head><nitf:body.content><bodyText><p nitf:lede="true">Today, it’s fairly common for the ultra-rich to pour their wealth into environmental causes.</p><p nitf:lede="true">Jeff Bezos, Tom Steyer and many other billionaires have donated portions of their riches to fight climate change and conserve nature.</p><p nitf:lede="true">But years before them, there was the man some consider the real “Captain Planet”: Ted Turner.</p><p nitf:lede="true">Turner, who died Wednesday at the age of 87, will be remembered as a buccaneering entrepreneur whose companies reshaped media, news, advertising and sports. But for all his business success, Turner’s efforts to protect the planet itself and its few remaining wild places could be his most enduring legacy.</p><p>Turner protected American prairies, helped restore iconic species and embraced renewable energy as a climate-change solution years before it was en vogue.</p><p>Larry Selzer, president and CEO of nonprofit The Conservation Fund, said Turner was in league with President Teddy Roosevelt as “one of the few leaders who reshaped how Americans think about the value, beauty and protection of our natural heritage.”</p><p>“Ted Turner changed the scale of what conservation could be,” Selzer said in a written statement. “He understood that protecting land wasn’t just about individual places — it was about thinking big enough to match the landscapes themselves.”</p><p>‘An example for others to follow’</p><p>As in business, Turner did indeed think big about land protection. The impact of his conservation is most visible in the American West.</p><p>Starting in the 1990s, he began buying chunks of land across the Western U.S., eventually amassing more than 2 million acres of land to become one of the largest landowners in North America.</p><p>Today, the land holdings overseen by his family business, Turner Enterprises, stretch across several states, from South Dakota to New Mexico. The land is privately owned, but huge swaths are managed to provide habitat for rare and endangered species that once thrived in the West.</p><p>It is the American bison, however, that Turner’s name has become synonymous with.</p><p>Turner said he’d been infatuated with bison, North America’s largest land mammal, since reading about them as a child. The hulking animals, which can weigh nearly as much as a small car, were hunted to the brink of extinction in the 1800s.</p><p>“When I was a little boy, about 10 years old, I read National Geographic Magazine, it had an article about bison and then it said how close they came to extinction,” Turner said in an interview from the 2019 CNN documentary, “Ted Turner: Captain Planet.”</p><p>“I decided then that if I could, that I would do what I could to help bring the bison back,” Turner said.</p><p>By the time Turner purchased his first bison in 1976, the species was in better shape, but populations were still far below their peak, when tens of millions once roamed the Great Plains.</p><p>With help from public and private partners, Turner played a pivotal role rebuilding the ranks of the iconic species.</p><p>Today, his ranches are home to 45,000 bison, making it the largest privately owned herd on the planet.</p><p>Turner’s land stewardship has also helped restore habitat for other rare and endangered species, from the cutthroat trout to the blackfooted ferret.</p><p>Adam Putnam, CEO of the waterfowl conservation nonprofit Ducks Unlimited, said that as one of the country’s most high-profile outdoorsmen, Turner “wielded his enormous influence in a positive way for large-scale habitat conservation, setting an example for others to follow.”</p><p>Putnam added that “his impact on wetlands and waterfowl … will be felt for generations to come.”</p><p>Turner on burning fossil fuels: ‘It’s poisonous’</p><p>Scientists have been sounding the alarm for decades about the threats posed by human-caused climate change. But among business leaders, the problem wasn’t always as widely discussed as it is today, at least not publicly.</p><p>Never one to shy away from sharing his views, Turner spoke openly about his climate worries — and pushed industry to embrace solutions.</p><p>Turner summed up his views on global warming during a 2010 interview at the Stanford Graduate School of Business, telling a crowd of students it was “time to say goodbye to coal and oil.”</p><p>“We’re burning too much of it and it’s poisonous,” Turner said. “And we’re going to run out of it anyway.”</p><p>Turner was a believer in clean energy and embraced renewables like solar through his business endeavors.</p><p>When he was asked by CNN’s Christiane Amanpour in a 2015 interview where he’d tell young entrepreneurs to invest their money and creativity, his answer was unequivocal: “clean, renewable energy.”</p><p>In 2011, he built one of Atlanta’s first solar canopies over a parking lot next to the downtown Atlanta headquarters for many of his family’s businesses. And through his Turner Renewable Energy subsidiary, he partnered in 2010 with Atlanta-based energy and electricity giant Southern Company to develop some of the company’s first large-scale solar developments.</p><p>‘We can’t afford to think that way anymore’</p><p>Turner also imbued his companies with his environmentalist ethos. Ever the businessman, he also managed to turn some of his conservation efforts into money-making ventures.</p><p>At outposts of his Ted’s Montana Grill restaurants, he reacquainted American diners with lean, sustainably raised bison as a meat source. The restaurants, which he co-founded with Atlanta restaurateur George McKerrow, also were early adopters of environmentally-friendly dining solutions, like paper straws and locally-sourced ingredients.</p><p>Turner turned several of his properties into ecotourism destinations under the umbrella of Ted Turner Reserves.</p><p>His Vermejo, Ladder, Armendaris and Sierra Grande properties in the Western U.S. offer guests the chance to witness bison migrations and other wildlife experiences. Revenue they generate is poured back into conservation initiatives, like protecting bighorn sheep and restoring rare trout habitat.</p><p>In a 2019 interview with Mountain Outlaw magazine, Turner said he didn’t believe you have to “trash the planet” to succeed in business.</p><p>“That mentality should have faded long ago, and truthfully, when it comes to the current state of our environment, we can’t afford to think that way anymore,” Turner said.</p><p>This coverage is supported by a partnership with Green South Foundation and Journalism Funding Partners. You can learn more and support our climate reporting by donating at AJC. com/donate/climate.</p><p>COMING SUNDAY</p><p>» How Ted Turner built CNN, the world’s first 24-hour news network with a home base in midtown Atlanta.</p></bodyText></nitf:body.content></nitf:body><metadata><dc:metadata xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/"><dc:identifier value.Title: Federal Energy Regulatory Commission: Public Service Company of New Mexico submits tariff filing per 35.17(b): Amendment to be effective 6/1/2023 under ER26-2005 Filing Type : 130

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-Y0V3-S4TN-G1XC-00000-00Federal Energy Regulatory Commission: Public Service Company of New Mexico submits tariff filing per 35.17(b): Amendment to be effective 6/1/2023 under ER26-2005 Filing Type : 1302026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisFederal Energy Regulatory Commission: Public Service Company of New Mexico submits tariff filing per 35.17(b): Amendment to be effective 6/1/2023 under ER26-2005 Filing Type : 130

Washington: Public Service Company of New Mexico has submitted the following document to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission:

Public Service Company of New Mexico414 Silver Ave., SWAlbuquerque, NM87102505-241-2849Adam.Alvarez@pnm.comMay 1, 2026Via eTariffMs. Debbie-Anne A. ReeseSecretaryFederal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, N.E Washington, D.C.20426Re: Public Service Company of New Mexico,Docket No. ER26-2005-___Filing Type: 130 – Amendment to Pending FilingREQUEST FOR SHORTENED COMMENT PERIODDear Secretary Reese:Public Service Company of New Mexico (“PNM”) hereby submits an amendment to itsOrder No. 8981 compliance filing that was filed on April 2, 2026,2 in the above captionedproceeding to correct a typographical errors, identify changes that were inadvertently excludedfrom “Attachment B – Redline of Tariffs” 3 and correct the eTariff record and eTariff effectivedate to June 1, 2026.4 In addition and to the extent necessary, PNM requests waiver of theCommission ’ s standard 60-day notice period to have an effective date of June 1, 2026. In light ofthis minimal amendment to the Compliance Filings, PNM also requests a shortened commentperiod of no more than ten (10) Calendar Days.I. CorrectionsTypographical ErrorPNM was made aware that the sub-bullets to the identified Template Changes on page 3of the transmittal letter were misidentified under the main bullets, and should be corrected to matchAttachment H-2 and to state:Order No. 898 Related Changes • FERC Form 1 Inputs:o PNM is adding “Energy Storage” and “Energy Storage AccumulatedDepreciation Reserve” and the FERC Form 1 References • Schedule 1 – Plant1 Acct. & Reporting Treatment of Certain Renewable Energy Assets, Order No. 898, 183 FERC ¶ 61,205(2023).2 PNM ’ s filing was received on April 1, 2026, at 5:00:33 PM.3 PNM is providing as Attachment B a marked tariff version of the Formula Rate Template.4 PNM's transmittal letter and Attachment H-7 correctly identified June 1, 2026, as the effective date.However, the template was incorrectly placed in the eTariff record under Attachment H-2 instead ofAttachment H-1, and it mistakenly listed June 1, 2023, as the effective date.Ms. Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 1, 2026Page 2o PNM is adding “Energy Storage” and “Energy Storage AccumulatedDepreciation Reserve” prior year ending, monthly, and current end of yearbalancesIn the updated template, PNM is addressing references to Form 1 that were finalized afterPNM submitted its own Form 1, as well as correcting naming conventions. Specifically, in theForm 1 Inputs Tab, the label “Average Production Plant in Service” has been revised to “AverageEnergy Storage Plant in Service. ” Additionally, in Schedule 1, the term “Average ProductionAccumulated Depreciation” was incorrectly labeled; this has now been corrected to “AverageEnergy Storage Accumulated Depreciation. ”Attachment B – Redline TariffPNM's Attachment B provided an incorrect version of the tariff and did not accuratelyreflect the changes mentioned in the transmittal letter. A revised Attachment B is included in thissubmission.Corrected eTariff Record and Effective DatePNM unintentionally applied the template changes to the incorrect eTariff record andmistakenly listed the year as 2023 instead of 2026. PNM is correcting the eTariff record andeffective date to June 1, 2026, as requested in the transmittal letter.II. Requested Effective Date, Request for Waiver, request for shortened commentperiod, and request for commission actionRequest for WaiverPNM respectfully requests that the Tariff changes requested herein be given an effectivedate of June 1, 2026, the same effective date as PNM ’ s annual formula rate update. Good causeexists for the Commission to grant waiver of its 60-day notice requirement and permit a June 1,2026, effective date because it would align the effective date of the formula rate template changeswith the date of PNM ’ s annual update.5 In addition, PNM respectfully requests that theCommission waive any portion of Part 35 of its regulations which has not been satisfied by thislimited filing.Request for Shortened Comment PeriodIn light of this minimal amendment to the Compliance Filings, PNM also requests ashortened comment period of no more than ten (10) Calendar Days. Good cause exists to grant ashortened comment period because PNM ’ s filing was not protested, and the amendments hereinare minor revisions that clarify PNM ’ s amendment to comply with Order No. 898. Thus, no entitywill be prejudiced by a shortened notice period.5 See 18 C.F.R §§ 35.3(a)(2) and 35.11; Prior Notice and Filing Requirements Under Part II of the FederalPower Act, 64 FERC ¶ 61,139 at 61,983-84 (1993); see also Central Hudson Gas and Electric Corp., 60FERC ¶ 61,106, reh ’ g denied, 61 FERC ¶ 61,089 (1992).Ms. Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 1, 2026Page 3Thank you for your attention to this matter. Please direct any questions concerning thissubmission to the undersigned.Sincerely,/s/ Adam J. AlvarezAdam J. AlvarezDirector, Regulatory Policy & Case ManagementPublic Service Company of New Mexico

The complete document can be viewed at this link: https://elibrary.ferc.gov

value.Title: REGION BRIEFS
value.Overview: Navajo Times (Window Rock, Arizona), NEWS; Pg. C2, 1153words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JKY-M0J3-SFTK-F4MS-00000-00REGION BRIEFS2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. C2REGION BRIEFS

Lane restrictions set on US 160

KAYENTA - Daytime lane restrictions are scheduled May 18-22 on U.S. Route 160 east of Kayenta as crews continue a pavement rehabilitation project in northeastern Arizona.

The restrictions will be in place from 6 a.m. to 6 p.m.

Monday through Thursday and from 6 a.m. to noon Friday. No weekend work is scheduled.

U.S. 160 will be reduced to one lane in 3-mile segments roughly between milepost 416 and milepost 440 near Dennehotso, Arizona. Traffic will alternate between eastbound and westbound travel through the work zone.

Flaggers and a pilot car will guide traffic while the restrictions are in place. Drivers should be ready for frequent stops near and inside the work zone, where crews and equipment will be working.

The restrictions will be lifted each day after work hours.

The restrictions are needed so crews can make pavement repairs along U.S. 160 inside the project area. The work is part of the $19.7 million U.S. 160 Dennehotso to Milepost 440 project.

The project is scheduled to be completed this summer.

DESRI to break ground on two San Juan County solar projects

KIRTLAND, N.M. - DESRI will break ground today, Thursday, May 14, on two solar and battery storage projects in San Juan County that are expected to produce enough energy to equal the yearly electricity use of more than 60,000 New Mexico homes.

The groundbreaking for Foxtail Flats Solar and Storage and Four Mile Mesa Solar and Storage is scheduled for 10:30 a.m.

Mountain time near Kirtland, New Mexico.

The projects mark the beginning of construction near the now-retired San Juan Generating Station, the coal-fired power plant that once operated in the region.

Together, Foxtail Flats and Four Mile Mesa represent 270 megawatts of solar energy and 180 megawatts of battery energy storage, according to information provided by DESRI, a renewable energy company.

Foxtail Flats has a 20-year power purchase agreement with the Incorporated County of Los Alamos. Four Mile Mesa has a 20-year power purchase agreement with Public Service Company of New Mexico.

People expected to attend the groundbreaking include DESRI leaders, representatives from Tierra Adentra Growth Capital, SOLV Energy, local community partners, Ute Mountain Ute Tribe leaders, San Juan County elected officials and PNM representatives.

The groundbreaking will be held at the project site near Kirtland, where visuals will include the ceremony, construction equipment and the nearby operating San Juan Solar and Storage facility.

Coconino County seeks input on hazard plan

FLAGSTAFF - The Coconino County Emergency Management is taking public comments through May 31 on a draft plan that identifies natural hazards and ways to reduce long-term risk in local communities.

The draft 2026 Coconino County Multi-Jurisdictional Hazard Mitigation Plan was released for public review this month. It includes participation from Coconino County, Flagstaff, Page, Williams, Tusayan, Fredonia, Northern Arizona University and other partner agencies.

The plan looks at natural hazards that could affect communities in the county and outlines ways to reduce vulnerability before disasters happen. Community members, businesses and other stakeholders may review the draft and submit feedback before the plan is finalized for adoption.

The draft plan is available at coconino.az.gov/2474/Hazard-Mitigation-Plan. Comments may be submitted by email at emergencymanagement@coconino.az.gov or online at coconino.az.gov/hmpfeedback.

Candidate packets available for Navajo County school board races

HOLBROOK, Ariz. - Navajo County residents planning to run for school district governing board seats can now pick up candidate packets from the county superintendent's office.

Rachael Lewis, the superintendent of schools for Navajo County, announced that several school district governing board seats will be on the ballot this year.

Candidate packets are available at the Navajo County Superintendent of Schools Office at 100 E. Code Talkers Drive in Holbrook. The packets are also available online through the county's candidate forms webpage.

People interested in running for a governing board seat must file a statement of interest with the Superintendent of Schools Office before they begin collecting petition signatures. The form is included in the candidate packet.

The official filing period for candidate nomination papers runs from Saturday, June 6, 2026, through Monday, July 6, 2026, at 5 p.m.

Because the first filing day falls on a Saturday, the Superintendent of Schools Office will begin accepting documents Monday, June 8. The office is open Monday through Thursday from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. and Friday from 8 a.m. to noon.

All candidates for school district governing board seats must file their paperwork with the Navajo County Superintendent of Schools Office.

The seats on the 2026 ballot include two seats each in Winslow, Joseph City, Pinon, Snowflake, Show Low, Whiteriver, Kayenta and Blue Ridge.

Holbrook has one seat open in Ward 1, one in Ward 2 and one in Ward 3. The Ward 3 term ends in December 2028.

Heber-Overgaard has two seats open, along with one seat with a term ending in December 2028. Cedar has two seats open, along with one seat with a term ending in December 2028.

The Northern Arizona Vocational Institute of Technology has one seat open for Winslow, one for Holbrook and one for Blue Ridge. The Northeast Arizona Technological Institute of Vocational Education has one Kayenta seat open with a term ending in December 2028.

Explora opens kindness exhibit

ALBUQUERQUE - Explora Science Center and Children's Museum of Albuquerque announced Tuesday the opening of a new exhibition that uses a fictional grandmother's home to teach visitors how kindness can be studied through science.

The exhibition, The Science of Kindness in "Grandma's Casita," is set inside Grandma's house. In the exhibit, Grandma is presented as a retired social scientist, traveler and New Mexican who shares stories of kindness from different parts of the world.

Visitors can take part in activities that examine the ripple effects of kindness, study how facial features show emotion and read research notes that show how kindness can be studied.

The exhibit connects kindness to neuroscience, psychology, sociology and anthropology.

The exhibition was funded by the Lilly Endowment Inc. through a children's museum initiative done with the Association of Children's Museums.

Explora is one of more than 20 museums in the multiyear effort.

The exhibit builds on earlier work at Explora focused on the science of kindness, funded by The Jennifer Riordan Foundation and its partners, along with a planning grant from the Lilly Endowment Inc.

Explora plans to take parts of the exhibition to six partner museums in New Mexico. The work funded by the five-year Lilly Endowment Inc. grant will later move into exhibits on teamwork and perseverance.

value.Title: SunState Solar Provides Access to Affordable Clean Energy Solutions in Albuquerque and Santa Fe

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-4WW3-RYBF-V3M7-00000-00SunState Solar Provides Access to Affordable Clean Energy Solutions in Albuquerque and Santa Fe2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisSunState Solar Provides Access to Affordable Clean Energy Solutions in Albuquerque and Santa Fe

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

May 12th, 2026 ( MarketersMedia News Hub - Delivered by Newstex )

SunState Solar is helping homeowners in Albuquerque transition to cost-saving, environmentally friendly energy with customized solar installations and flexible financing options. With local expertise, 5-star service, and comprehensive support from consultation to installation, the company makes switching to solar simple and accessible.

Albuquerque, New Mexico, United States, May 13, 2026 -- According to the Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA), solar accounted for 54% of all new electricity-generating capacity added to the US grid in 2025. Solar and storage made up 79% of new capacity in the same year, and SEIA expects cumulative US solar capacity to nearly triple from year-end 2025 to 2036.

As more people are becoming aware of the planet's climate crisis, they're increasingly turning to renewable energy that leaves as small a carbon footprint as possible. As shown in the above statistics, solar energy is a particularly popular option, as it can be cleanly harvested from a natural resource.

Solar energy should be something for homeowners in Albuquerque to consider, especially with recent rises in energy costs. While there are significant upfront costs to consider, ultimately, going solar can drastically lower utility bills, especially when combined with tax credits and incentives (e.g., 30% federal solar investment tax credit (ITC), as well as state and local incentives).

For many people, the systems pay for themselves within just a few years; homeowners can lock in low energy costs for 25+ years, too. On top of that, they enjoy net meter benefits, where they sell excess energy back to the grid for credits.

SunState Solar is here to provide installation services for both homeowners and businesses, and these services are backed by its 100% 5-star ratings on Google and other platforms. Every system comes with a 25-year warranty and worry-free installation, as the business is committed to delivering top-quality solar solutions.

This is a locally owned and community-focused business, which sets them apart from large and impersonal solar companies. SunState Solar exclusively serves the Albuquerque and Santa Fe metro areas, which means that the team thoroughly knows local energy needs. The company also gives back to the community through charitable contributions, community involvement, and partnerships that create lasting positive change.

Homeowners who need financial assistance with the upfront cost can utilize SunStates Solar's zero-down financing and flexible payment plans. For example, they can benefit from $0 down payment plans, as well as low/affordable monthly payments. They can also make use of the company's many financing partners.

Interested parties can take advantage of SunState Solar's free step-by-step guide to understanding everything solar. This can help them make well-informed decisions confidently when switching to solar.

They can also schedule a free consultation with this business. During this meeting, they'll go over current energy bills and energy needs by assessing the roof and energy usage. With this information, the company can design a custom solar system that meets and exceeds those requirements.

Once the installation starts, it's all in SunState Solar's hands. Its team will handle the permits and paperwork, making it a stress-free process for homeowners.

Other services that SunState Solar offers are solar inspections, pest barriers, solar panel cleaning, troubleshooting and repair, and solar monitoring.

About the company: SunState Solar is a locally owned and operated solar installation company based in New Mexico, providing residential and commercial solar solutions throughout Albuquerque, Santa Fe, and surrounding areas.

value.Title: Southern Company Chairman, President and CEO Chris Womack Regarding the Passing of Ted Turner

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHF-20B3-RWRK-G1G2-00000-00Southern Company Chairman, President and CEO Chris Womack Regarding the Passing of Ted Turner2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisSouthern Company Chairman, President and CEO Chris Womack Regarding the Passing of Ted Turner

SOUTHERN CO ("SO-N") - Southern Company Chairman, President and CEO Chris - Womack Regarding the Passing of Ted Turner Southern Company Chairman, President and CEO Chris Womack said: "Ted Turner was a visionary who called Atlanta homean entrepreneur whose bold ideas helped transform our city into a global media hub and whose legacy resonates far beyond our region. Many people knew Ted as a pioneer in media and broadcasting, but he was also a passionate advocate for environmental stewardship, working alongside Southern Company to help pioneer the development of utility-scale solar energy. Through our partnership with Turner Enterprises and Turner Renewable Energy, we advanced several projects including Cimarron Solar, our first solar facility constructed in 2010 and built on Ted's New Mexico property, as well as Campo Verde Solar, and Morelos del Sol Solar in California.

These projects helped Southern Company establish its first utility-scale solar operations, expand our development expertise, and accelerate the integration of solar into our generation portfolio. Ted's pioneering spirit and unwavering commitment to making a difference changed the world for the better, inspiring countless individualsincluding myselfto pursue innovation with purpose and passion. On behalf of Southern Company, I extend my deepest condolences to his family, friends and all who were touched by his remarkable life and leadership. Atlanta, and indeed the world, is better for it." About Southern Company Southern Company (NYSE:SO) is a leading energy provider serving 9 million customers across the Southeast and beyond through its family of companies. The company has electric operating companies in three states, natural gas distribution companies in four states, a competitive generation company, a leading distributed energy solutions provider with national capabilities, a fiber optics network and telecommunications services. Our uncompromising values ensure we put the needs of those we serve at the center of everything we do and are the key to our sustained success, driven by nearly 30,000 employees dedicated to delivering exceptional service. To learn more, visitwww.southerncompany.com.

value.Jurisdiction: New Mexico
value.Title: OPINION: Why local electrical workers support Blackstone's acquisition of TXNM Energy

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-5SG3-RYK8-H1SR-00000-00OPINION: Why local electrical workers support Blackstone's acquisition of TXNM Energy2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisOPINIONOPINION: Why local electrical workers support Blackstone's acquisition of TXNM EnergyFonze Martinez Jr.

For decades, the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers Local 611 (IBEW 611) members have powered New Mexico in every storm, heat wave, and through every major transition in our state's energy landscape. We know what it takes to keep the lights on. We also know what it looks like to work with a company that respects its workforce and invests in the future. Historically, IBEW and Public Service Company of New Mexico have had a strong, mutually beneficial working relationship. Together, we've navigated grid modernization, wildfire risks, renewable integration and major storms. Throughout this time, PNM has honored our labor agreements, valued union craftsmanship and kept decision-making rooted in New Mexico.

History matters. We are grateful for the relationships we've built with PNM, and ensuring this dynamic continues well into the future is paramount. With Blackstone Infrastructure's commitment to PNM maintaining local control and direct accountability to IBEW 611 workers and our customers, we are proud to stand in support of their acquisition of TXNM Energy, the parent company of PNM. Blackstone's proposed agreement also commits to honoring all union labor agreements. In an era when out-of-state corporate takeovers often mean hollowed-out workforces and outsourced expertise, this deal safeguards the local, unionized workforce that knows New Mexico best.

New Mexico's energy grid needs massive, long-term investment. Blackstone brings exactly that: patient, long-term capital designed to strengthen New Mexico's energy infrastructure. PNM is already working toward a carbon-free future, integrating resources that supply more than two-thirds of its electricity from carbon-free energy. But achieving full reliability, modernization and compliance with our state's Energy Transition Act requires resources, and this acquisition directly supports our ability to meet these resource needs.

For IBEW 611 members, this acquisition means steady work, better tools, safer equipment and the certainty that long-planned projects won't be delayed due to inadequate funding. This stability is critical for our workforce to thrive and communities to flourish.

Supporting this acquisition means recognizing strong benefits to our workers and New Mexico, while insisting on continued public oversight and accountability. After years of uncertainty around PNM's ownership, this partnership provides something workers and residents desperately need: stability, investment and a clear path toward a resilient energy future.

We will continue tracking the regulatory proceedings closely, and supporting this acquisition does not mean a blank check for anyone. However, in the agreements outlined in the acquisition proposal, Blackstone is prepared to commit the resources to help PNM modernize the grid, responsibly integrate renewable energy and maintain reliability, all while keeping PNM firmly rooted in New Mexico and preserving its long-standing, productive relationship with IBEW.

IBEW Local 611 stands in support of the Blackstone–PNM acquisition. This deal will strengthen union jobs, keep decision-making local, protect customers, invest in communities and put New Mexico at the forefront of America's clean energy future.

We cannot afford to let politics or fear derail an opportunity this strong. With eyes wide open and regulators holding all parties to their commitments, this partnership gives our state the tools we need to build a more resilient, equitable, and union-powered future.

Fonze Martinez Jr. is the business manager and financial secretary for International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers Local 611.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGN-K9B3-S3VB-5351-00000-00New Mexicans should determine energy future2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisAPg. 10New Mexicans should determine energy futureLetters to the Editor

The future of energy for New Mexico should be determined by New Mexicans, not multinational corporations. Blackstone is not a utility company that cares about delivering safe, clean, reliable energy to our state. Rather, the company is in the business of making money and sees an opportunity to diversify its massive financial portfolio to ensure a steady stream of profit to shareholders.Don't be fooled by the idea that a state public regulatory agency will keep it in check. Publicly regulated private utility companies routinely get away with unfair rate hikes and deferred infrastructure maintenance. Let alone commit to reaching the goal of 100% renewables, the failure of which is now purely political as the science and engineering of renewables support this transition. Blackstone is not committed to a just and equitable transition to a renewable energy future.

Keep our public utility a public utility.

Dan Antonioli

Santa Fe

Desired result

In response to Ray Rodriguez's latest letter ("Vance-Trump '28," Letters to the Editor, April 29), I am quite pleased to see the Resist signage in my front yard is, indeed, having its intended effect.

David Pierce

Santa Fe

Restaurant city

Thank you, Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham, for restoring the food service inspections ("Gov. orders food service inspections to resume," April 30). Some of these inspections were true horror stories. It will be a pleasure to resume eating at restaurants that play by the rules. Along with the arts, Santa Fe is a restaurant city.

Felicia Rocca

Santa Fe

Vote Greene

I am proud to endorse Justin Greene for our community as county commissioner, District 1. For decades, Greene has demonstrated a deep commitment to Santa Fe through thoughtful leadership, creative problem-solving and a genuine dedication to the people who call this place home. From helping revitalize the Railyard to supporting spaces like Warehouse 21, Justin has consistently invested in projects that uplift our community, especially our youth and small businesses. His work reflects a clear understanding that strong communities are built through collaboration, opportunity and inclusive vision.

At a time when we need steady, experienced leadership, Greene brings both the track record and the values to move us forward. He listens, he shows up and he delivers. He will continue to advocate for policies that strengthen our local economy, support working families and preserve what makes Santa Fe unique. I encourage my neighbors to join me in supporting Justin Greene.

Sam Levy

Santa Fe

The three Cs

Classic Clueless Conservative: That describes GOP gubernatorial candidate Duke Rodriguez to a T. Clearly, Duke has his eye focused squarely on New Mexico's multibillion-dollar permanent funds. Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham has it precisely correct; he is indeed despicable to even consider a lawsuit intended to deny working families childcare support that would allow them and New Mexico as a whole to thrive instead of struggle ("Judge pauses governor's free childcare program," April 29).

I have to shake my head and wonder what kind of upbringing this Classic Clueless Conservative had that would make him believe his actions are in any way justified by common decency, much less by any religious traditions. Cruelty toward the less fortunate is despicable. I call for Rodriguez to hang his head in shame and repent for his cruelty.

Joe Eduardo

Santa Fe

value.Title: Colorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy production

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JMS-VYB3-S9KK-F4G3-00000-00Colorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy production2026-05-28T16:00:51Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisENVIRONMENTColorado governor, western leaders eye multistate plan to boost geothermal energy productionRobert Tann Follow rtann@summitdailynews.com

Gov. Jared Polis said Colorado plans to be part of a multistate effort to boost geothermal energy production in the West.

Polis joined Utah Gov. Spencer Cox on Wednesday, May 20, to unveil the partnership between Colorado, Utah, Arizona and New Mexico. Speaking virtually at the event, which was held in downtown Salt Lake City, Polis said greater investment in geothermal energy will "save people money on energy bills; provide reliable, 24/7 energy; create jobs in both tech and on-site; (and) protect clean air."

"Ninety-five percent of America's capacity for geothermal is located in the western states, and it's underutilized," Polis said. "It's there, and it's ready, and we want to pursue it."

Cox said the focus of the initiative will be on building a geothermal energy workforce and attracting investment.

"If you bring Colorado on board and Arizona on board and New Mexico and all of these companies … now we've got something big that's going to attract a lot of attention and can help us move much more quickly," Cox said.

Cox also hopes that the group can serve as a conduit between the federal government and local projects when it comes to issues like permitting. Much of the land in the West is federally owned, meaning states that want to unlock more geothermal projects will likely need buy-in from the federal government. In Colorado, the federal government owns over a third of the state's land.

A multistate alliance, through its delegates in Congress, will help bolster support in Washington for geothermal projects, Cox said.

Geothermal projects grow across western Colorado

Geothermal energy is derived from heat energy that comes from the Earth's core and is pulled to the surface. A 2024 report by the Colorado Energy and Carbon Management Commission identified three broad uses for geothermal energy.

This includes direct use that does not require energy conversion, such as hot springs, hot water and space heating. Other uses are geothermal power production, which is generated by converting thermal energy into electricity, and geoexchange, which includes using underground pipes to tap into the Earth's thermal energy to heat and cool structures above the surface.

Colorado has invested millions of dollars in recent years in geothermal projects many of which are concentrated across the Western Slope through a combination of state grant and tax credit programs.

The Aspen School District in April secured $5 million to install thermal energy infrastructure designed to heat and cool several buildings, while the town of Vail received close to $2 million in February to build out a geothermal network using sites like the public library. Other recent state funding has gone to the town of Hayden, which netted nearly $1 million to help install ground-source heat pumps across various sites, and $30,000 to help the Memorial Hospital in Craig study geothermal energy for its facilities.

As Colorado pushes more communities to wean off fossil fuels as part of its goal to reach 100% net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, some local leaders have looked to geothermal as a key part of their energy portfolio.

Vail town leaders hope investments in geothermal infrastructure will help reduce town-wide emissions by more than 10,000 metric tons annually, while officials in Hayden have floated geothermal energy as a replacement for coal to keep Hayden Station, a coal plant set for retirement, running.

Michael O'Connor, director for the newly-formed multistate group, dubbed the Mountain West Geothermal Consortium, said untapped geothermal energy could provide over 200 gigawatts of "affordable, baseload power" in western Rockies states, based on the findings of a U.S. Department of Energy study.

That represents over a quarter of current peak energy demand for the entire lower 48 states in the U.S., which is around 760 gigawatts, or 760,000 megawatts.

"It's not a commitment of any sort, but it's the size of the prize," O'Connor said during Wednesday's event in Salt Lake City.

Bill passed to incentivize more geothermal development

The multistate alliance comes on the heels of legislative action in Colorado to incentivize more geothermal energy production.

Senate Bill 142, which passed during this year's legislative session, would require investor-owned utility providers to include geothermal energy as part of their overall energy resource plan, which they submit to the Colorado Public Utilities Commission every few years. Utilities would have to solicit proposals to develop small-scale geothermal projects of up to 25 megawatts of net capacity, and large-scale projects with more than 25 megawatts of capacity.

Current law requires local governments that build or acquire gas or electric energy systems to first get voter approval. The bill passed by lawmakers removes the need for voter approval for geothermal heating and cooling systems. It would also allow local governments to issue bonds to help pay for thermal energy infrastructure.

Another provision of the bill allows industrial or commercial facilities that recover thermal energy as a by-product of their operations to sell that energy, which would not be subject to regulation by the Public Utilities Commission.

"Geothermal has been around for a long time, but it's an area that is now receiving a lot of attention," said Sen. Matt Ball, a Denver Democrat and one of the bill's lead sponsors, during the legislation's first committee hearing in April.

Ball said Colorado is well-positioned to be a leader in geothermal energy. Much of the energy production requires digging wells, jobs that can be filled from the state's oil and gas industry. Colorado also ranks as one of the top states geologically for natural geothermal resources, like hot springs.

"It's really an industry and a space that has a ton of promise, and it's one that I think we as a state should really be leaning into," Ball said.

The bill passed the Senate and House with broad bipartisan support and has been sent to Polis to be signed into law.

value.Jurisdiction: U.S. Federal; International; New Mexico; Texas
value.Title: US Sets Record Energy Production Driven by Natural Gas, Oil, Renewables

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-3D13-RWVY-G41S-00000-00US Sets Record Energy Production Driven by Natural Gas, Oil, Renewables2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisUS Sets Record Energy Production Driven by Natural Gas, Oil, Renewables

Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, according to new data in Monthly Energy Review released by the US Energy Information Administration (EIA).

Total production was driven by record-high production in natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids (NGPLs), and renewables.

This was the fourth consecutive year in which the United States set a record for total energy production, the report noted.

Dry natural gas production grew more than 4% from 2024 to a record-high 39 trillion cubic feet in 2025, with most of the growth occurring in the Appalachia, Permian, and Haynesville regions.

Natural gas has been the largest source of US domestic energy production since 2011, and the United States has been the largest natural gas producer in the world since 2011.

Oil production

Crude oil production also set a record 13.6 million barrels per day in 2025 and grew by 3%, or 350,000 barrels per day, compared with the previous record set in 2024.

Most of that growth occurred in the Permian region of western Texas and southeastern New Mexico.

Crude oil accounted for 26% of domestic energy production, and the United States remained the largest crude oil producer in the world.

Production of NGPLs, which are hydrocarbons separated as liquids during natural gas processing, grew 7% to a record 4 trillion cubic feet in 2025 compared with the previous record set in 2024.

In 2025, NGPLs accounted for 9% of domestic energy production. NGPL production has grown every year since 2005 as natural gas production and processing have increased.

Renewables

Renewable energy production grew by 3% from 2024 to a new record, the fifth consecutive year of growth.

Solar and wind both set records for energy production as new generators came online.

Geothermal, hydroelectric, and wood and waste energy production remained steady from 2024 to 2025. Biofuels production, which had grown in four consecutive years, declined slightly.

Coal

Coal accounted for 10% of domestic energy production in 2025. It increased by 4% from 2024 levels to 533 million short tonnes in 2025 after two years of declining production.

urn:contentItem:6JK3-G5T3-RYC4-W0B6-00000-00United States set record energy production in 20252026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisUnited States set record energy production in 2025

13 May 2026 (Wam) Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, according to new data in Monthly Energy Review released by the US Energy Information Administration.

Total production was driven by record-high production in natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids (NGPLs), and renewables. This was the fourth consecutive year in which the United States set a record for total energy production, the report noted.

Dry natural gas production grew more than 4% from 2024 to a record-high 39 trillion cubic feet in 2025, with most of the growth occurring in the Appalachia, Permian, and Haynesville regions. Natural gas has been the largest source of US domestic energy production since 2011, and the United States has been the largest natural gas producer in the world since 2011.

Crude oil production also set a record 13.6 million barrels per day in 2025 and grew by 3%, or 350,000 barrels per day, compared with the previous record set in 2024. Most of that growth occurred in the Permian region of western Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Crude oil accounted for 26% of domestic energy production, and the United States remained the largest crude oil producer in the world.

Production of NGPLs, which are hydrocarbons separated as liquids during natural gas processing, grew 7% to a record 4 trillion cubic feet in 2025 compared with the previous record set in 2024. In 2025, NGPLs accounted for 9% of domestic energy production. NGPL production has grown every year since 2005 as natural gas production and processing have increased.

Renewable energy production grew by 3% from 2024 to a new record, the fifth consecutive year of growth. Solar and wind both set records for energy production as new generators came online. Geothermal, hydroelectric, and wood and waste energy production remained steady from 2024 to 2025. Biofuels production, which had grown in four consecutive years, declined slightly.

Coal accounted for 10% of domestic energy production in 2025. It increased by 4% from 2024 levels to 533 million short tons in 2025 after two years of declining production.

value.Title: United States set record energy production in 2025

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-FRB3-S9DG-S4VH-00000-00United States set record energy production in 20252026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisUnited States set record energy production in 2025TF

WASHINGTON, 13th May, 2026 (WAM) -- Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, according to new data in Monthly Energy Review released by the US Energy Information Administration.

Total production was driven by record-high production in natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids (NGPLs), and renewables. This was the fourth consecutive year in which the United States set a record for total energy production, the report noted.

Dry natural gas production grew more than 4% from 2024 to a record-high 39 trillion cubic feet in 2025, with most of the growth occurring in the Appalachia, Permian, and Haynesville regions. Natural gas has been the largest source of US domestic energy production since 2011, and the United States has been the largest natural gas producer in the world since 2011.

Crude oil production also set a record 13.6 million barrels per day in 2025 and grew by 3%, or 350,000 barrels per day, compared with the previous record set in 2024. Most of that growth occurred in the Permian region of western Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Crude oil accounted for 26% of domestic energy production, and the United States remained the largest crude oil producer in the world.

Production of NGPLs, which are hydrocarbons separated as liquids during natural gas processing, grew 7% to a record 4 trillion cubic feet in 2025 compared with the previous record set in 2024. In 2025, NGPLs accounted for 9% of domestic energy production. NGPL production has grown every year since 2005 as natural gas production and processing have increased.

Renewable energy production grew by 3% from 2024 to a new record, the fifth consecutive year of growth. Solar and wind both set records for energy production as new generators came online. Geothermal, hydroelectric, and wood and waste energy production remained steady from 2024 to 2025. Biofuels production, which had grown in four consecutive years, declined slightly.

Coal accounted for 10% of domestic energy production in 2025. It increased by 4% from 2024 levels to 533 million short tons in 2025 after two years of declining production.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-8WH3-S75N-T0BT-00000-00THE UNITED STATES SET RECORD ENERGY PRODUCTION IN 2025, AGAIN2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:00:51ZLexisNexisTHE UNITED STATES SET RECORD ENERGY PRODUCTION IN 2025, AGAINStates News ServiceWASHINGTON, DC

The following information was released by the Energy Information Administration (EIA):

Data source: U.S. Energy Information Administration, Monthly Energy Review

Data values: Primary Energy Production by Source

Note: NGPL=Natural gas plant liquids

Total energy production in the United States increased to a new record of 107 quadrillion British thermal units (quads) in 2025, a 3.4% increase from the previous record set in 2024, according to new data in our Monthly Energy Review. Total production was driven by record-high production in natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids (NGPLs), and renewables. This was the fourth consecutive year in which the United States set a record for total energy production.

Dry natural gas production grew more than 4% from 2024 to a record-high 39 trillion cubic feet in 2025, with most of the growth occurring in the Appalachia, Permian, and Haynesville regions. Natural gas has been the largest source of U.S. domestic energy production since 2011, and the United States has been the largest natural gas producer in the world since 2011.

Crude oil production also set a record 13.6 million barrels per day in 2025 and grew by 3%, or 350,000 barrels per day, compared with the previous record set in 2024. Most of that growth occurred in the Permian region of western Texas and southeastern New Mexico. Crude oil accounted for 26% of domestic energy production, and the United States remained the largest crude oil producer in the world.

Production of NGPLs, which are hydrocarbons separated as liquids during natural gas processing, grew 7% to a record 4 trillion cubic feet in 2025 compared with the previous record set in 2024. In 2025, NGPLs accounted for 9% of domestic energy production. NGPL production has grown every year since 2005 as natural gas production and processing have increased.

Data source: U.S. Energy Information Administration, Monthly Energy Review

Data values: Renewable Energy Production and Consumption by Source

Renewable energy production grew by 3% from 2024 to a new record, the fifth consecutive year of growth. Solar and wind both set records for energy production as new generators came online. Geothermal, hydroelectric, and wood and waste energy production remained steady from 2024 to 2025. Biofuels production, which had grown in four consecutive years, declined slightly.

Coal accounted for 10% of domestic energy production in 2025. It increased by 4% from 2024 levels to 533 million short tons in 2025 after two years of declining production.

We convert sources of energy to common units of heat, called British thermal units, to compare different types of energy that are usually measured in units that are not directly comparable, such as barrels of crude oil and cubic feet of natural gas. Appendix A of our Monthly Energy Review has the conversion factors that we use for each energy source.

Principal contributor: Brett Marohl

value.Title: Who was Ted Turner? Media mogul dead at 87
value.Overview: Detroit Free Press Online (Michigan), ENTERTAINMENT: LATEST, ENTERTAINMENT: LATEST & NEWSPAPER PUBLISHING NEWS, 432words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-CNJ3-S80X-K431-00000-00Who was Ted Turner? Media mogul dead at 872026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:47:05ZLexisNexisENTERTAINMENT: LATEST, ENTERTAINMENT: LATEST & NEWSPAPER PUBLISHING NEWSWho was Ted Turner? Media mogul dead at 87Jenna Prestininzi and Bryan Alexander, USA TODAY NETWORK

Media mogul Ted Turner, known for founding CNN and Turner Classic Movies, died Wednesday, May 6, at age 87.

Turner died with his family around him, according to a Turner Enterprises press release. A prominent media businessman and philanthropist, his empire expanded to include CNN, TCM, Cartoon Network, TNT, TBS, Ted's Montana Grill, the Turner Foundation and other ventures.

"Ted was an intensely involved and committed leader, intrepid, fearless and always willing to back a hunch and trust his own judgement," Mark Thompson, chairman and CEO of CNN Worldwide, said in a statement. "He was and always will be the presiding spirit of CNN. Ted is the giant on whose shoulders we stand, and we will all take a moment today to recognize him and his impact on our lives and the world."

Turner is survived by his five children, fourteen grandchildren and two great-grandchildren.

"A private family service is planned, and a public memorial will be held at a later date, with details to follow," Turner Enterprises said in its statement.

Here's what to know about Ted Turner.

When did Ted Turner die?

Ted Turner died Wednesday, May 6, at 87, "surrounded by his family," according to a Turner Enterprises news release obtained by USA TODAY.

Who was Ted Turner?

Born Robert Edward Turner III in Cincinnati and raised in Georgia, known as Ted Turner, he was a businessman, media mogul and philanthropist known for founding CNN as the first global 24-hour cable news station in 1980, according to Turner Enterprises.

Turner also founded the television stations TBS, TCM, TNT and Cartoon Network and launched two independent film production companies.

Turner was married to actress and activist Jane Fonda for 10 years, from 1991 until the couple divorced in 2001.

He started Ted's Montana Grill, an American restaurant chain specializing in Montana bar-style, serving beef or bison burgers alongside cold brews. His Ted Turner Reserves offer a tranquil stay surrounded by New Mexico's great outdoors.

Delving into sports, Turner was a longtime owner of the Atlanta Braves and the Atlanta Hawks.

His philanthropical legacy included chairing the Turner Foundation, which donated to environmental causes and promoted sustainable energy, and establishing the Turner Renewable Energy company, Turner Enterprises noted. Turner also founded the United Nations Foundation and the Nuclear Threat Initiative, advocating for global peace and prosperity.

USA TODAY contributed.

Contact Jenna Prestininzi: jprestininzi@freepress.com.

This article originally appeared on Detroit Free Press: Who was Ted Turner? Media mogul dead at 87

May 6th, 2026 ( Source New Mexico - Delivered by Newstex )

Ted Turner, once New Mexico's largest landowner, earned praise from New Mexico's elected officials and conservationists for his work. He died May 6, 2026, at the age of 87. He's pictured above addressing the Newsmaker Luncheon on renewable and alternative energy at the National Press Club on April 19, 2011, in Washington, DC. (Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images)

New Mexico conservationists and elected officials on Wednesday issued statements celebrating the conservation legacy of Ted Turner, who was once New Mexico's largest landowner and made the state a model of private land conservation.

GET THE MORNING HEADLINES.

SUBSCRIBE

Turner, 87, died early Wednesday morning, according to a statement from his family. Turner's estate owns more than 1 million acres across four sprawling ranches in northern and southern New Mexico. His purchase of the Vermejo Ranch in 1996 established a precedent for how private benefactors can be conservation leaders and save endangered species, said Garrett VeneKlasen, director of New Mexico Wild, in a phone interview with Source NM on Wednesday.A bighorn sheep looks out from a ridge on Ted Turner's Armendaris Ranch in New Mexico in this undated photo. Turner died May, 6, 2026, according to his family. (Photo courtesy Ted Turner Reserves)

'He was a New Mexico conservation icon,' VeneKlasen said. 'As a landowner, he was one of the most exemplary landowners in terms of stewarding big landscapes and big chunks of habitat.'

VeneKlasen said Turner was instrumental in protecting species including the Rio Grande cutthroat trout, the Mexican gray wolf, the Bolson tortoise and the Chiricahua leopard frog. Jonathan Hayden, executive director of the New Mexico Land Conservancy, also said Turner's efforts helped save the bighorn sheep, as well as the bats that used his Armendaris Ranch as a stop on their migratory path.

'All the bison reintroduction, too,' Hayden told Source NM on Wednesday. 'That has been a huge kind of catalyst for thinking about how to reintroduce native species to an eco-region that was kind of long overlooked.'

New Mexico elected officials also publicly praised Turner's impact and offered condolences to his family.

'His Vermejo Ranch became one of the great conservation success stories in American history: half a million acres restored from coal mining and industry back to thriving wilderness, with bison, pronghorn, and clean streams to prove it,' Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham said in a post to social media Wednesday. 'New Mexico is better for what Ted left behind.'

U.S. Sen. Martin Heinrich (D-N.M.) called Turner the 'real-life Captain Planet' in a statement to social media on Wednesday. 'I will miss his leadership, and my thoughts are with his family and friends,' he wrote.

Turner founded the news network CNN and, later in life, turned his attention to philanthropy and conservation. After Vermejo, Turner increased his holdings across the West, becoming one of the country's biggest landowners with more than 2 million acres across eight Western states.

'He was also a man known for his no-holds-barred delivery, endearing sense of humor, and undying loyalty to those around him,' according to the statement from his family Wednesday.

Link to the original story.
